title: Greedy Algorithms
description: Making the locally best choice at every step and hoping (correctly, for the right problems) it leads to a globally best answer.
objectives: Understand the greedy strategy: locally optimal choices, never reconsidered
Solve activity selection and coin change with greedy approaches
Recognize when greedy fails and a different strategy is needed

sections.text: An approach that makes the choice that looks best RIGHT NOW, at each step, without reconsidering it later, hoping this sequence of local choices produces a globally optimal result.

Greedy does NOT try every possibility like Backtracking did -- it commits immediately and never looks back. This makes greedy algorithms fast, but they only give the CORRECT answer for problems that have a specific property (covered below) -- using greedy on the wrong problem gives a wrong answer, confidently.

Given start/end times for several activities, select the MAXIMUM number that don't overlap. The greedy insight: always pick the activity that finishes EARLIEST among the remaining options -- this leaves the most room for future activities.

sections.title: Tracing after sorting by end time
steps: Original activities already happen to be in end-time order: [1,3] (end 3), [2,5] (end 5), [4,6] (end 6), [6,8] (end 8), [5,9] (end 9)
[4,6]: start 4 >= lastEnd 3 -- take it! count=2, lastEnd=6
[6,8]: start 6 >= lastEnd 6 -- take it! count=3, lastEnd=8

sections.text: Arrays.sort(activities, Comparator.comparingInt(a -> a[1])) uses a Comparator (Comparable vs Comparator) with a lambda to sort a 2D array of intervals by their END time (index 1) -- this sorting step is what makes the greedy 'earliest finish first' rule actually work.
3. Greedy Coin Change (Works for SOME Coin Systems)

sections.title: Tracing minCoinsGreedy({1,5,10,25}, 41)
steps: i=3 (coin 25): 41>=25 -- take one, amount=16, count=1. 16<25 -- stop this coin
i=2 (coin 10): 16>=10 -- take one, amount=6, count=2. 6<10 -- stop
i=1 (coin 5): 6>=5 -- take one, amount=1, count=3. 1<5 -- stop
i=0 (coin 1): 1>=1 -- take one, amount=0, count=4. 0<1 -- stop
amount==0 -- return count=4 (one 25, one 10, one 5, one 1)

sections.text: Greedy coin change ONLY gives the correct (minimum) answer for certain coin systems, like standard currency (1, 5, 10, 25). For an ARBITRARY coin system -- say coins {1, 3, 4} needing to make 6 -- greedy picks 4 then 1 then 1 (three coins: 4+1+1), but the true minimum is 3+3 (two coins)! This is a classic example of greedy FAILING -- the real solution for arbitrary coin systems needs Dynamic Programming, covered starting in the next lesson.

sections.title: The property greedy problems need
points: Greedy choice property: a locally optimal choice at each step leads to a globally optimal solution -- this must be PROVABLE for the specific problem, not just assumed
Optimal substructure: an optimal solution to the whole problem contains optimal solutions to its sub-problems (this property is ALSO required by Dynamic Programming, covered next)
If a problem lacks the greedy choice property, greedy will produce a WRONG answer, sometimes without any obvious sign something went wrong

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming greedy always works because it 'seems reasonable' -- it must be specifically PROVEN correct for that problem; intuition alone is not proof.
Using greedy coin change on an arbitrary coin system and trusting the result -- it only guarantees correctness for specific systems like standard currency denominations.
Forgetting to sort by the correct criterion (like end time for activity selection) before applying the greedy rule -- the greedy choice depends on processing items in the right order.
Confusing greedy (commit immediately, never reconsider) with backtracking (try, and UNDO if it doesn't work out) -- they're fundamentally different strategies.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a greedy algorithm?
questions.answer: An approach that makes the locally best choice at each step, without reconsidering it later, hoping the sequence of local choices produces a globally optimal result -- unlike backtracking, greedy never undoes a decision.

questions.question: Why does greedy activity selection sort by END time rather than start time or duration?
questions.answer: Picking whichever remaining activity finishes earliest leaves the maximum possible room for future activities to also fit without overlapping -- sorting by start time or duration doesn't guarantee this same property.

questions.question: Does greedy coin change always give the minimum number of coins? Give a counterexample if not.
questions.answer: No -- only for specific coin systems like standard currency. For coins {1, 3, 4} and amount 6, greedy picks 4+1+1 (three coins), but the true minimum is 3+3 (two coins). Arbitrary coin systems require Dynamic Programming for a guaranteed correct answer.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Greedy algorithms commit to the locally best choice at each step and never reconsider it, unlike backtracking's try-then-undo approach. This works correctly only for problems with the greedy choice property, provable case by case -- activity selection (always pick the earliest-finishing remaining activity) is a genuine example. Greedy coin change works for standard currency denominations but fails for arbitrary coin systems (e.g. {1,3,4} making 6: greedy gives 3 coins, but 2 is possible) -- such cases require Dynamic Programming, which explores overlapping sub-problems more carefully instead of committing blindly.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Greedy = grab the best-looking option RIGHT NOW and never look back -- fast, but only safe when you've PROVEN local best = global best. When in doubt, greedy is a guess dressed up as a strategy.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What defines a greedy algorithm's strategy?
Why does activity selection's greedy rule sort by end time specifically?
Give a coin system where greedy coin change fails.
What property must a problem have for greedy to guarantee a correct answer?

sections.title: Assign Cookies
sections.problemStatement: Each child i has a greed factor g[i] (minimum cookie size to be content). Each cookie j has size s[j]. Assign at most one cookie per child to maximize the number of content children.

examples.explanation: Only cookie size 1 can satisfy child with greed 1; no cookie is big enough for greed 2 or 3.

hints: Greedily give the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy unsatisfied child -- using a bigger cookie than necessary wastes it.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort greed factors and cookie sizes. Two pointers: advance the cookie pointer until it can satisfy the current child, then move both pointers forward (child satisfied); otherwise just advance the cookie pointer.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n + m log m)

sections.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
sections.problemStatement: Given daily stock prices, find the maximum profit from one buy followed by one later sell.
hints: Greedily track the minimum price seen so far, and at each day compute profit = price - minSoFar, keeping the best.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Single pass, tracking the minimum price seen so far and the best profit achievable by selling at the current day.

sections.title: Jump Game
sections.problemStatement: Given an array where nums[i] is the max jump length from index i, return whether you can reach the last index starting from index 0.

examples.explanation: Index 3 has value 0, trapping you there -- index 4 is unreachable regardless of earlier choices.

hints: Greedily track the farthest reachable index so far.
If the current index ever exceeds the farthest reachable index, you're stuck.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track farthest reachable index while scanning left to right; if the current index i is beyond what's reachable, return false early. Update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]).

sections.title: Jump Game II
sections.problemStatement: Same setup as Jump Game, but return the MINIMUM number of jumps needed to reach the last index (guaranteed reachable).

examples.explanation: Jump from index 0 to index 1 (value 3), then from index 1 to index 4.

hints: Greedy BFS-like layering: track the farthest reachable index using the CURRENT jump's range, and the boundary of that range.
When you reach the boundary of the current jump's range, you must take another jump -- increment the counter and extend the boundary to the farthest seen.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track currentEnd (farthest reachable using jumps taken so far) and farthest (best reachable including the node being examined). When i reaches currentEnd, a new jump is forced: increment jumps and advance currentEnd to farthest.

sections.title: Gas Station
sections.problemStatement: n gas stations in a circle, gas[i] fuel available at station i, cost[i] fuel needed to travel to the next station. Return the starting station index from which you can complete the full circuit, or -1 if impossible (the answer is guaranteed unique if it exists).

examples.explanation: Starting at stations 0, 1, or 2, the running tank goes negative (each has gas - cost = -2). Starting at station 3: tank = 4 - 1 = 3, then + 5 - 2 = 6 at station 4 -- never goes negative, completing the circuit.

hints: If total gas < total cost, it's impossible overall -- return -1.

Otherwise, a solution always exists. Greedily track a running tank total; whenever it drops below 0, none of the stations from the previous start through the current one can be a valid start -- reset the candidate start to the next station.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track total surplus (gas - cost summed over all stations) and a running local tank. Whenever the local tank goes negative at station i, no start in [candidateStart, i] can work, so reset candidateStart to i+1 and zero the tank. If total surplus is negative, return -1; otherwise candidateStart is the answer.

sections.title: Partition Labels
sections.problemStatement: Given a string, partition it into as many parts as possible such that each letter appears in at most one part. Return the sizes of these parts.

examples.explanation: First part 'ababcbaca' (9 chars) contains all occurrences of a, b, c. Next 'defegde' (7 chars) contains all of d, e, f, g. Last 'hijhklij' (8 chars) contains all of h, i, j, k, l.

hints: Precompute the LAST index of each character.
Scan left to right, extending the current partition's end to the max(current end, last-index of each new character seen). Close the partition when the scan index reaches that end.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: First pass: record the last occurrence index of each character. Second pass: track the end of the current partition as the max last-index seen so far; when the scan index reaches that end, the partition is complete -- record its size and start a new one.

sections.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
sections.problemStatement: Given a collection of intervals, return the minimum number of intervals to remove so that the rest are non-overlapping.

examples.explanation: [1,3] overlaps both [2,3] and would need removal; removing just [1,3] leaves [1,2],[2,3],[3,4] non-overlapping.

hints: This is exactly Activity Selection in reverse: sort by END time, greedily keep an interval if it starts >= the last kept interval's end; count removals as intervals that must be dropped.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort intervals by end time. Greedily keep the first interval; for each subsequent interval, if it starts before the last kept interval's end (overlap), it must be removed (increment removal count); otherwise keep it and update the last-kept end.
